Military Apr 9, 2026

From 19 B-2 Spirits To 145 B-21 Raiders: How Much Bigger America's Stealth Bomber Fleet Is About To Get

A $4.5 billion production deal signal the Air Force's commitment to building its next-generation bomber...

The B-21 production scale represents a 7.6x expansion of stealth bomber capability, indicating strategic pivot toward peer-competitor deterrence over legacy platform sustainment.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Asiana Airlines to be Taken Over by Korean Air 2026

According to local news outlet Tomato News, Korean Air is planning to acquire the remaining 36.1% stake in Asiana Airlines. It owns 63.9% currently and much control in the management of it. But it wants to form a single airline structure, gradually removing the airline as a brand, starting in...

Korean Air's full acquisition of Asiana signals industry consolidation in Asia-Pacific, eliminating a major competitor and creating operational efficiency through brand integration by 2026.

See Travel Radar →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

Federal Aviation Administration's Crippling Shortage Risks Mass Flight Delays & Safety

More than 5,100 accidents had a narrative that air traffic control was a factor or probable cause.

With over 5,100 accidents attributed to ATC factors, understaffing at the FAA poses systemic risks beyond operational delays, threatening aviation's safety foundation.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Airbus Delivered 60 New Planes In March, But Overall Q1 Output Lags 16% From Last Year

Pratt & Whitney engine delays are now the clearest drag on Airbus’ 2026 delivery performance.

Airbus faces a structural bottleneck as Pratt & Whitney engine supply constraints will define production ceilings through 2026, potentially impacting aircraft financing and airline schedules.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

Operators must demonstrate systematic risk management processes across operations, not just compliance checkboxes, to satisfy FAA's Safety Management System expectations.

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Ouch: American Airlines Joins 4 Other US Carriers In Raising Checked Bag Fees

American Airlines becomes the latest US carrier to increase checked bag fees for basic economy tickets.

American Airlines' fee increase signals industry-wide margin pressure as carriers seek incremental revenue from ancillary services rather than base fares.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

The Growth of Regional Airlines this 2026

Regional carriers are gaining traction as major airlines cut capacity or reshuffle their networks due to U.S.–Iran conflict and the surge in fuel prices. This is creating opportunities for smaller operators to serve underserved markets with short‑haul flights. New Routes and Network Expansion in...

Regional carriers are strategically positioned to capture market share as majors contract capacity, with geopolitical disruptions and fuel volatility making smaller operators' cost-efficient short-haul models increasingly competitive.

See Travel Radar →

Safety Apr 9, 2026

Why Airlines Prefer Window Shades To Be Open During Taxi, Takeoff, & Landing

This is a simple safety concept, but not all airlines agree.

Open window shades enable crew and passengers to detect external hazards and assess emergency situations faster, making it a critical but inconsistently enforced safety protocol across carriers.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

How to Assess Safety Risk

A practical walkthrough of safety risk assessment under 14 CFR Part 5.

Part 5's structured risk assessment framework enables operators to systematically quantify hazards and prioritize mitigation strategies before incidents occur.

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Indoor Testing Facilities available at the NASA Unmanned Autonomy Research Complex (NUARC)

Windshaper A large WindShaper fan array is available for dynamic low-speed and hovering flight research. The WindShaper is ideal for generating arbitrary wind gradients and wind gusts via a simple Python API. A companion WindProbe is also available for quick surveys of flows. The WindProbe utilizes...

NASA's NUARC provides researchers controlled indoor wind simulation capabilities enabling rapid prototyping and testing of autonomous aircraft without weather delays or safety restrictions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

I Am Artemis: Dan Florez

Listen to this audio excerpt from Dan Florez, test director for NASA’s Exploration Ground Systems Program: At 1:47 a.m. EST November 16, 2022, as the Artemis I engines ignited, NASA astronaut Reid Wiseman and Dan Florez, NASA test director for the agency’s Exploration Ground Systems, watched from...

Ground systems test directors are critical mission enablers whose real-time decisions during launch sequences directly impact the success of NASA's deep-space exploration programs.

See NASA →

Safety Apr 9, 2026

Frontier Airlines Pilots Slam Brakes On Busy LAX Taxiway: “2 Trucks Just Cut Us Off”

The near-miss has sparked concerns about vehicle transponders, following the deadly Air Canada crash last month.

Ground vehicle detection technology gaps at major hubs pose systemic collision risks that transcend individual airport incidents and demand industry-wide transponder standardization.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 9, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Medium-sized regional airports like CID serve as critical infrastructure hubs by accommodating diverse aircraft types from trainers to air carriers, enabling pilot development and regional connectivity simultaneously.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

The Airbus A350 Is Known As The World's Extra Widebody, But Soon It Won’t Be

The Boeing 777X is set to be the widest passenger jet in production, although the A350-1000 appears set to retain the record for maximum exit limit.

Boeing's 777X will surpass the A350's cabin width, forcing Airbus to defend its capacity advantage through higher passenger density and exit configuration optimization.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Indoor Testing Facilities available at the NASA Unmanned Autonomy Research Complex (NUARC)

WindShaper A large WindShaper fan array is available for dynamic low-speed and hovering flight research. The WindShaper is ideal for generating arbitrary wind gradients and wind gusts via a simple Python API. A companion WindProbe is also available for quick surveys of flows. The WindProbe utilizes...

NASA's WindShaper facility enables researchers to simulate complex wind conditions for autonomous aircraft testing without outdoor flight risks, accelerating development cycles for low-speed and hovering vehicle validation.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Only 238 Seats: What Qantas Sacrificed To Make 22-Hour Nonstop Flights Possible

Project Sunrise flights are coming soon.

Qantas reduced cabin capacity by roughly 40% on its 787-9s to achieve ultra-long-range capability, demonstrating that route viability trumps seat density for strategic market positioning.

See Simple Flying →

▶
Safety Apr 9, 2026

RNO Mustang 9 Departure

Pilots departing from runways 17 left and right at Reno-Tahoe International need to pay special attention to the Mustang 9 Departure. It is extremely important that the procedure on the Mustang 9...

The Mustang 9 departure requires heightened pilot attention due to specific procedural demands that differentiate it from standard RNO departures.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

United Airlines increases checked bag fees

United Airlines has become the latest airline to increase the checked baggage fee, effective immediately, in response to rising fuel costs. Other airlines, including Delta Air Lines, Qantas, and Air Canada, have also increased ticket prices to manage fuel shortages. Check-in luggage cost in United...

Coordinated fee increases across major carriers signal airlines are shifting fuel cost pressures to passengers rather than absorbing them operationally.

See Travel Radar →

General Aviation Apr 9, 2026

EAA Aviation Foundation Awards 127 Scholarships for 2025-26

Flight training and post-secondary awards total more than $800,000.

The EAA's $800K+ scholarship commitment signals sustained industry investment in pilot pipeline development amid ongoing workforce shortage concerns.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Starstruck

The Artemis II crew captured this photo of our galaxy, the Milky Way, on April 7, 2026. The Milky Way’s elegant spiral structure is dominated by just two arms wrapping off the ends of a central bar of stars. Spanning more than 100,000 light-years, Earth is located along one of the galaxy’s spiral...

Artemis II's orbital photography demonstrates advanced imaging capabilities relevant to high-altitude aerospace documentation and observation systems.

See NASA →

Industry Apr 9, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Integrated operational optimization across airlines, airports, and air traffic systems offers a cost-effective path to handle predicted traffic growth while reducing environmental impact.

See MDPI →

General Aviation Apr 9, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 9, 2026

2015 Lockwood AirCam over the Palisades Reservoir.

The Lockwood AirCam's continued presence in recreational flying demonstrates the enduring appeal of affordable, high-visibility aircraft for leisure pilots despite competition from modern designs.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

House Set To Vote on ALERT Act Next Week

Measure would direct FAA rulemaking on collision-avoidance systems, airspace procedures and military operations.

Congressional mandate could accelerate FAA standards for detect-and-avoid technology critical to integrating unmanned and advanced aircraft into controlled airspace.

See AVweb →

Military Apr 9, 2026

US Navy Pilots In Training Will Now Make Their 1st Carrier Landing In An F/A-18 Or F-35

No tailhook required to earn wings of gold?

Eliminating the T-45 trainer removes a critical proficiency gate, potentially accelerating pilot pipeline throughput but risking readiness for carrier operations' demanding physics.

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Watching the Artemis II Mission Unfold at JPL’s Space Flight Operations Facility

Description Staff at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California watch the agency’s Artemis II mission unfold soon after launch on April 1, 2026, at the Space Flight Operations Facility, which operates the Deep Space Network (DSN). The DSN comprises of three complexes in Goldstone,...

JPL's Deep Space Network infrastructure demonstrates critical ground support capabilities required for deep space missions beyond lunar orbit.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

The Deep Space Network Acquires Artemis II Signal

Description A graphical representation of the Deep Space Network’s radio frequency antennas indicate signal acquisition from NASA’s Artemis II mission to the Moon on April 1, 2026, inside the Space Flight Operations Facility at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California. Two antennas...

The Deep Space Network's successful signal acquisition demonstrates critical ground infrastructure capability for deep space missions beyond low Earth orbit operations.

See NASA →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

FAA Pilot Population Climbs Again In 2025

Latest Civil Airmen Statistics show the strongest gains from student pilots.

Student pilot growth signals potential long-term relief for the pilot shortage crisis, though workforce pipeline maturation will take years to translate into available commercial and airline crew capacity.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

The Deep Space Network Acquires Artemis II Signal

Description The acquisition of the radio frequency signal from the Artemis II crewed mission to the Moon by NASA’s Deep Space Network (DSN) is indicated by the peak in the data signal shown on the top computer screen. Soon after the mission’s launch on April 1, 2026, at 6:35 p.m. EDT, NASA’s Near...

NASA's Deep Space Network successfully locked onto Artemis II's signal post-launch, demonstrating critical ground infrastructure capability for sustained lunar mission communications.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Watching Over the Deep Space Network Before Artemis II Signal Acquisition

Description Blanca Renteria, Artemis Deep Space Network (DSN) operations chief, monitors data at the Space Flight Operations Facility at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California shortly after Artemis II launched from the agency’s Kennedy Space Flight Center in Florida on April 1,...

NASA's Deep Space Network requires specialized ground operations infrastructure to maintain continuous communication with lunar missions, representing critical support systems beyond launch vehicles themselves.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Watching the Artemis II Launch From JPL’s Space Flight Operations Facility

Description Staff at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California watch the launch of the agency’s Artemis II mission to the Moon on April 1, 2026, at the Space Flight Operations Facility, which operates the Deep Space Network (DSN). Soon after launch, the Artemis II crew communicated...

JPL's Deep Space Network will provide critical real-time communications and tracking for Artemis II, extending NASA's lunar mission capabilities beyond Earth orbit operations.

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

JPL’s ‘Lucky Peanuts’ Before Artemis II Launch

Description A container of “lucky peanuts” sits above workstations within the Space Flight Operations Facility at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory shortly before the launch of the Artemis II mission to the Moon on April 1, 2026. Eating peanuts before launches and other major mission events is a...

NASA's JPL maintains the tradition of consuming lucky peanuts before major missions as a psychological morale ritual that has preceded successful launches since the 1960s.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

American Airlines Plans To Resume Flights Between Miami & Venezuela This Month

After a six-year hiatus, American Airlines is poised to reignite flights between the US and Venezuela, with a daily Miami-Caracas route.

The resumption of US-Venezuela air service signals potential normalization of diplomatic relations and unlocks revenue opportunities for carriers in underserved Caribbean markets.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

PLD Space secures $35 million European Investment Bank loan for MIURA 5 launcher

MILAN – PLD Space signed a 30 million euro ($35 million) venture debt loan with the European Investment Bank (EIB) on April 7, a move to support the final development stage of MIURA 5, PLD’s lightweight rocket. Miura 5, which is designed to deploy roughly 540 kilograms to 1 ton of payload to low...

European public financing for commercial launch vehicles signals institutional confidence in small-lift orbital competition and reduces reliance on private venture capital.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Council Cracks Down on Problematic Parking at Bristol Airport

North Somerset Council has taken steps to intensify enforcement action against unauthorised and problematic parking at Bristol Airport and the surrounding area. This forms part of its broader Parking Action Plan. A collective effort is being made by officials at North Somerset Council, Bristol...

Increased parking enforcement at Bristol Airport could reduce ground congestion and improve airport operations while generating revenue through penalty notices.

See Travel Radar →

Safety Apr 9, 2026

Cape Air flight forced to return to airport after cabin door opens mid-flight

A Cape Air flight was forced to return to the airport shortly after takeoff when the aircraft’s cabin door partially opened mid-air. The aircraft was bound for Boston Logan International Airport from Nantucket Memorial Airport in Massachusetts when the alarming but ultimately casualty-free incident...

The incident underscores recurring maintenance and preflight inspection vulnerabilities in regional carriers operating high-frequency short routes where turnaround pressures may compromise safety protocols.

See Travel Radar →

Drones Apr 9, 2026

Skyrover wants to be your next DJI drone

Skyrover, a relatively new but increasingly visible drone brand, is stepping into a tense and uncertain US market, and it’s doing so with a very clear message: we’re here to stay. Here’s what that means for American drone buyers right now. more…...

Skyrover's market entry signals intensifying competition in consumer drones as regulatory clarity improves, potentially disrupting DJI's dominant US position through localized alternatives.

See DroneDJ →
